news_summary: Progress in human rights is marked by strengthened protections for women’s sports, affirming consent in legislation, upholding asylum rights, and expanding voting access for individuals with felony convictions.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès en matière de droits de l'homme se traduisent par un renforcement de la protection du sport féminin, l'inscription du principe du consentement dans la législation, la garantie du droit d'asile et l'élargissement du droit de vote aux personnes ayant fait l'objet d'une condamnation pénale.
news_summary_es: Los avances en materia de derechos humanos se caracterizan por el refuerzo de las protecciones para el deporte femenino, la consagración del consentimiento en la legislación, la defensa del derecho de asilo y la ampliación del derecho al voto para las personas condenadas por delitos graves.

articles.title: FIFA: New Rule Recognizes Afghan Women’s Team
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Players of Afghan Women's United football team receive support from Tunisian players after the FIFA Unites: Women's Series 2025 on October 29 in Casablanca, Morocco.
                    © 2025 Francois Nel - FIFA/FIFA via Getty Images
          
    


  
(Vancouver) – The FIFA Council’s decision to approve amendments to its Governance Regulations provides a landmark opportunity to ensure gender equity and human rights in sport, the Sport & Rights Alliance (SRA) said today. These changes allow for the official recognition of the Afghanistan Women’s National Team in exile, ensuring that the players can once again represent their country in official FIFA competitions.“For five years, we were told the Afghanistan Women’s National Team could never compete again because the men who took our country would not allow it,” said Khalida Popal, founder and director of Girl Power, and former captain and cofounder of the Afghanistan Women’s National Team. “I am extremely proud of this decision by FIFA and glad that our collective advocacy has not only changed the future for Afghan women, but also ensured that no other national team has to sacrifice what our players did. This is the rebirth of hope and a strong message to those who try to erase women from society: you will not succeed. Women belong on the pitch, in public life, and everywhere decisions are made.”The decision, approved on April 29, 2026, at the FIFA Council, grants FIFA the authority, in consultation with the relevant confederation, to register national teams for official competitions when their home member association is “unable to do so.”“This FIFA decision is critical to ensuring every Member Association upholds their responsibilities toward gender equity and human rights,” said Andrea Florence, executive director of the Sport & Rights Alliance. “This is about more than just football: it’s about sending a message that no government should have the power to erase women from public life. We are thrilled that FIFA has listened to Afghan women and addressed this gap in their statutes. We look forward to cheering them on for years to come.”After the Taliban took over Afghanistan in August 2021 and subsequently banned all women and girls from sport, the Afghanistan Women’s National Team has lived and trained in exile, scattered among Albania, Australia, Portugal, the United Kingdom, and the United States. Despite their demonstrated resilience, the team members were blocked from official competitions because FIFA rules required the approval of the Taliban-controlled Afghanistan Football Federation. The new amendment effectively ends this requirement.“FIFA has finally done the right thing by closing the loophole that allowed the Taliban’s discriminatory policies to be enforced on the global stage,” said Minky Worden, director of global initiatives at Human Rights Watch. “FIFA’s action should serve as a model for how international sports bodies should respond when athletes are systemically excluded because of their gender, ethnicity, or beliefs.”In a pivotal report published in March 2025, “It's Not Just a Game: It's Part of Who I Am,” the Sport & Rights Alliance outlined the Afghanistan Women’s National Team’s case for recognition, noting that the continued exclusion of the team represented a breach of FIFA’s nondiscrimination and gender equity mandates. FIFA’s subsequent formation of the Afghan Women United refugee team provided a partial solution, but the decision opens the pathway to grant the players full status and participation as a national squad.“Afghan women have been punished twice: once by the Taliban who drove them from their homes, and again by global sports bodies that let them fall through the cracks,” said Steve Cockburn, head of economic and social justice at Amnesty International. “Official recognition of the women’s football team will represent a step toward justice for all Afghan women, and proof of what can be achieved when the international community refuses to look away.”The Sport & Rights Alliance extends its deepest gratitude to all the players, fans, coaches, and activists worldwide whose tireless advocacy made this day possible. This victory belongs to the players, but its impact will be felt far beyond the pitch, setting a definitive precedent that women and girls belong in sport, and everywhere they choose to be.Quotes from Players:“For the last few years, we have played under many names—as refugees, as ‘Afghan Women United,’ and as guests of other clubs—but in our hearts, we were always the National Team. To hopefully be able to wear our flag again officially is a feeling I cannot describe.”–Nazia Ali, Afghan Women United, Australia“This achievement today honours the long and painful journey we have taken as Afghan women footballers, fighting discrimination, abuse, and harassment simply for the right to play the sport we love. Many of us have made enormous sacrifices, losing our homes, our country, our careers, and precious years of our footballing lives in the struggle for dignity and freedom. Yet we refused to give up. Today is not only about recognition; it is about securing our future. This team will no longer be a temporary or symbolic project: it will be permanent. Through our resilience and the sacrifices of so many players, we are sending a clear message to the world: Afghan women are here to stay.”–Sevin Azimi, Afghan Women United, United Kingdom“Today, the Taliban’s power no longer extends to the world of international football. It is not the end of gender apartheid in our country, but it is a sign that the fight is not over. When we take the pitch again as the Afghanistan Women’s National Team, we are sending a message to our sisters that we are with them, and that nothing is impossible.”–Maryam Karimyar, Afghan Women United, Portugal
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Players of Afghan Women's United football team receive support from Tunisian players after the FIFA Unites: Women's Series 2025 on October 29 in Casablanca, Morocco. © 2025 Francois Nel - FIFA/FIFA via Getty Images

(Vancouver) – The FIFA Council’s decision to approve amendments to its Governance Regulations provides a landmark opportunity to ensure gender equity and human rights in sport, the Sport & Rights Alliance (SRA) said today. These changes allow for the official recognition of the Afghanistan Women’s National Team in exile, ensuring that the players can once again represent their country in official FIFA competitions.

“For five years, we were told the Afghanistan Women’s National Team could never compete again because the men who took our country would not allow it,” said Khalida Popal, founder and director of Girl Power, and former captain and cofounder of the Afghanistan Women’s National Team. “I am extremely proud of this decision by FIFA and glad that our collective advocacy has not only changed the future for Afghan women, but also ensured that no other national team has to sacrifice what our players did. This is the rebirth of hope and a strong message to those who try to erase women from society: you will not succeed. Women belong on the pitch, in public life, and everywhere decisions are made.”

The decision, approved on April 29, 2026, at the FIFA Council, grants FIFA the authority, in consultation with the relevant confederation, to register national teams for official competitions when their home member association is “unable to do so.”

“This FIFA decision is critical to ensuring every Member Association upholds their responsibilities toward gender equity and human rights,” said Andrea Florence, executive director of the Sport & Rights Alliance. “This is about more than just football: it’s about sending a message that no government should have the power to erase women from public life. We are thrilled that FIFA has listened to Afghan women and addressed this gap in their statutes. We look forward to cheering them on for years to come.”

After the Taliban took over Afghanistan in August 2021 and subsequently banned all women and girls from sport, the Afghanistan Women’s National Team has lived and trained in exile, scattered among Albania, Australia, Portugal, the United Kingdom, and the United States. Despite their demonstrated resilience, the team members were blocked from official competitions because FIFA rules required the approval of the Taliban-controlled Afghanistan Football Federation. The new amendment effectively ends this requirement.

“FIFA has finally done the right thing by closing the loophole that allowed the Taliban’s discriminatory policies to be enforced on the global stage,” said Minky Worden, director of global initiatives at Human Rights Watch. “FIFA’s action should serve as a model for how international sports bodies should respond when athletes are systemically excluded because of their gender, ethnicity, or beliefs.”

In a pivotal report published in March 2025, “It's Not Just a Game: It's Part of Who I Am,” the Sport & Rights Alliance outlined the Afghanistan Women’s National Team’s case for recognition, noting that the continued exclusion of the team represented a breach of FIFA’s nondiscrimination and gender equity mandates. FIFA’s subsequent formation of the Afghan Women United refugee team provided a partial solution, but the decision opens the pathway to grant the players full status and participation as a national squad.

“Afghan women have been punished twice: once by the Taliban who drove them from their homes, and again by global sports bodies that let them fall through the cracks,” said Steve Cockburn, head of economic and social justice at Amnesty International. “Official recognition of the women’s football team will represent a step toward justice for all Afghan women, and proof of what can be achieved when the international community refuses to look away.”

The Sport & Rights Alliance extends its deepest gratitude to all the players, fans, coaches, and activists worldwide whose tireless advocacy made this day possible. This victory belongs to the players, but its impact will be felt far beyond the pitch, setting a definitive precedent that women and girls belong in sport, and everywhere they choose to be.

Quotes from Players:

“For the last few years, we have played under many names—as refugees, as ‘Afghan Women United,’ and as guests of other clubs—but in our hearts, we were always the National Team. To hopefully be able to wear our flag again officially is a feeling I cannot describe.”

–Nazia Ali, Afghan Women United, Australia

“This achievement today honours the long and painful journey we have taken as Afghan women footballers, fighting discrimination, abuse, and harassment simply for the right to play the sport we love. Many of us have made enormous sacrifices, losing our homes, our country, our careers, and precious years of our footballing lives in the struggle for dignity and freedom. Yet we refused to give up. Today is not only about recognition; it is about securing our future. This team will no longer be a temporary or symbolic project: it will be permanent. Through our resilience and the sacrifices of so many players, we are sending a clear message to the world: Afghan women are here to stay.”

–Sevin Azimi, Afghan Women United, United Kingdom

“Today, the Taliban’s power no longer extends to the world of international football. It is not the end of gender apartheid in our country, but it is a sign that the fight is not over. When we take the pitch again as the Afghanistan Women’s National Team, we are sending a message to our sisters that we are with them, and that nothing is impossible.”

–Maryam Karimyar, Afghan Women United, Portugal
articles.reasoning: The article reports a landmark decision by FIFA to officially recognize the Afghanistan Women’s National Football Team in exile, overcoming discriminatory bans imposed by the Taliban. This change has significant real-world impact by advancing gender equity and human rights in sport on a global scale. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights the broader symbolic and practical implications for women's rights and inclusion worldwide.
articles.personality_title: FIFA officially recognizes Afghanistan women's football team in exile
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Since the Taliban took control of Afghanistan in 2021, women and girls have been banned from playing sports in the country. The Afghanistan Women’s National Football Team was forced to live and train abroad but could not compete officially because the Taliban-controlled football federation blocked their participation.

**What happened**
On April 29, 2026, the FIFA Council changed its rules to allow national teams to compete even if their home football association cannot register them. This change officially recognizes the Afghanistan Women’s National Team in exile, allowing them to represent their country in FIFA competitions again.

**Impact**
This decision removes the barrier that kept the Afghan women’s team from playing on the world stage. It sends a clear message that discrimination based on gender will not be accepted in international sports. The team members, scattered across several countries, can now wear their country’s flag officially and compete as a national team.

**What's next step**
The Afghan Women’s National Team will prepare to participate fully in FIFA competitions. This recognition could inspire other sports organizations to support athletes facing discrimination and help protect women’s rights in sports globally.

**One-sentence takeaway**
FIFA’s new rule lets the Afghanistan Women’s National Football Team compete officially again, marking a major step for gender equality and human rights in sports.
articles.personality_title_fr: La FIFA reconnaît officiellement l’équipe féminine d’Afghanistan en exil
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Depuis la prise de pouvoir des talibans en Afghanistan en 2021, les femmes et les filles sont interdites de pratiquer le sport dans le pays. L’équipe nationale féminine afghane a dû vivre et s’entraîner à l’étranger, mais ne pouvait pas participer officiellement aux compétitions car la fédération contrôlée par les talibans bloquait leur inscription.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Le 29 avril 2026, le Conseil de la FIFA a modifié ses règles pour permettre aux équipes nationales de concourir même si leur association nationale ne peut pas les inscrire. Cette modification reconnaît officiellement l’équipe féminine d’Afghanistan en exil, leur permettant de représenter à nouveau leur pays lors des compétitions FIFA.

**Impact**
Cette décision supprime l’obstacle qui empêchait l’équipe afghane de jouer sur la scène internationale. Elle envoie un message clair contre la discrimination de genre dans le sport. Les joueuses, dispersées dans plusieurs pays, peuvent désormais porter officiellement les couleurs de leur pays et jouer comme équipe nationale.

**Prochaine étape**
L’équipe féminine afghane se préparera à participer pleinement aux compétitions FIFA. Cette reconnaissance pourrait encourager d’autres organisations sportives à soutenir les athlètes victimes de discrimination et à protéger les droits des femmes dans le sport à travers le monde.

**Une phrase clé**
La nouvelle règle de la FIFA permet à l’équipe féminine d’Afghanistan de rejouer officiellement, une avancée majeure pour l’égalité des sexes et les droits humains dans le sport.
articles.personality_title_es: La FIFA reconoce oficialmente al equipo femenino de fútbol de Afganistán en el exilio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Desde que los talibanes tomaron el control de Afganistán en 2021, las mujeres y niñas tienen prohibido practicar deportes en el país. El equipo nacional femenino de Afganistán tuvo que vivir y entrenar en el extranjero, pero no podía competir oficialmente porque la federación controlada por los talibanes bloqueaba su participación.

Qué pasó
El 29 de abril de 2026, el Consejo de la FIFA modificó sus reglas para permitir que los equipos nacionales compitan incluso si su asociación local no puede registrarlos. Este cambio reconoce oficialmente al equipo femenino de Afganistán en el exilio, permitiéndoles representar a su país en competencias FIFA nuevamente.

**Impacto**
Esta decisión elimina la barrera que impedía que el equipo femenino afgano jugara en el escenario mundial. Envía un mensaje claro contra la discriminación por género en el deporte. Las jugadoras, que están repartidas en varios países, ahora pueden vestir oficialmente la bandera de su país y competir como equipo nacional.

**Próximo paso**
El equipo femenino de Afganistán se preparará para participar completamente en competencias FIFA. Este reconocimiento podría motivar a otras organizaciones deportivas a apoyar a atletas que enfrentan discriminación y ayudar a proteger los derechos de las mujeres en el deporte a nivel mundial.

**Una frase clave**
La nueva regla de la FIFA permite que el equipo femenino de Afganistán compita oficialmente otra vez, un paso importante para la igualdad de género y los derechos humanos en el deporte.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant football pitch glowing softly under a gentle sunrise, with a group of diverse female silhouettes united in a circle at the center, their scarves and jerseys subtly patterned with the Afghan flag’s colors, surrounded by supportive, intertwined hands and footballs symbolizing solidarity, resilience, and the reclaiming of their place in sport.

articles.title: EU: Parliament Confirms That ‘Only Yes Means Yes’
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              A demonstration with the slogan "Without consent, it's rape" takes place in Rome, Italy, on February 28, 2026.
                    © 2026 Andrea Ronchini/NurPhoto via AP Photo 
          
    


  
The European Parliament adopted on Tuesday a resolution calling on the European Commission to make consent the determining factor in rape legislation across the EU, with 447 members voting “for,” 160 “against,” and 43 abstaining.Stating that “affirmative, freely given and unambiguous indication of consent is valid and that silence, a lack of verbal or physical resistance or the absence of a ‘no’ cannot be interpreted as consent,” the resolution also highlights that “consent can be withdrawn at any time before or during a sexual act.”The European Parliament also called on the European Union to take an intersectional, victim-centered approach, highlighting the need for improved services and support for survivors including sexual and reproductive health care, trauma care, psychological support, and access to safe abortions.As of today, 17 of the 27 EU member states already have consent-based rape laws, with other member states still defining rape based on elements of “force,” “threat,” or “coercion.” Since 2023, several European countries such as France,Finland, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands have adopted consent-based legislation, while Italy’s attempted legislative reform stalled.The adoption of an EU-wide standard on the legal definition of rape with consent at the core would align with international standards, including the EU’s obligations under the Istanbul convention, which states that “consent must be given voluntarily as the result of the person’s free will assessed in the context of the surrounding circumstances.”The United Nations special rapporteur on violence against women, Dubravka Šimonović, highlighted in a 2021 report that governments should harmonize legislation with international standards, stating that a “[l]ack of consent by the victim should be at the center of all definitions of rape.” Globally, rapes are widely underreported; a key factor is “criminal justice systems which define rape based on the use of force,” the report said.The EU member states who do not have consent-based definitions of rape in their domestic legislation do not need to wait to see if the European Commission proposes legislation, but instead can and should take immediate steps to amend their laws accordingly. It is past time to make clear that sex absent consent is rape.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image A demonstration with the slogan "Without consent, it's rape" takes place in Rome, Italy, on February 28, 2026. © 2026 Andrea Ronchini/NurPhoto via AP Photo

The European Parliament adopted on Tuesday a resolution calling on the European Commission to make consent the determining factor in rape legislation across the EU, with 447 members voting “for,” 160 “against,” and 43 abstaining.

Stating that “affirmative, freely given and unambiguous indication of consent is valid and that silence, a lack of verbal or physical resistance or the absence of a ‘no’ cannot be interpreted as consent,” the resolution also highlights that “consent can be withdrawn at any time before or during a sexual act.”

The European Parliament also called on the European Union to take an intersectional, victim-centered approach, highlighting the need for improved services and support for survivors including sexual and reproductive health care, trauma care, psychological support, and access to safe abortions.

As of today, 17 of the 27 EU member states already have consent-based rape laws, with other member states still defining rape based on elements of “force,” “threat,” or “coercion.” Since 2023, several European countries such as France,Finland, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands have adopted consent-based legislation, while Italy’s attempted legislative reform stalled.

The adoption of an EU-wide standard on the legal definition of rape with consent at the core would align with international standards, including the EU’s obligations under the Istanbul convention, which states that “consent must be given voluntarily as the result of the person’s free will assessed in the context of the surrounding circumstances.”

The United Nations special rapporteur on violence against women, Dubravka Šimonović, highlighted in a 2021 report that governments should harmonize legislation with international standards, stating that a “[l]ack of consent by the victim should be at the center of all definitions of rape.” Globally, rapes are widely underreported; a key factor is “criminal justice systems which define rape based on the use of force,” the report said.

The EU member states who do not have consent-based definitions of rape in their domestic legislation do not need to wait to see if the European Commission proposes legislation, but instead can and should take immediate steps to amend their laws accordingly. It is past time to make clear that sex absent consent is rape.
articles.reasoning: The European Parliament's resolution to make consent the defining factor in rape legislation across the EU represents a significant human rights advancement with broad societal impact. It promotes victim-centered legal standards and improved survivor support services, aligning with international norms and potentially harmonizing laws across multiple countries to better protect individuals from sexual violence.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament pushes for consent-based rape laws across the EU
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In many European Union (EU) countries, laws about rape differ. Some define rape by whether force or threats were used, while others focus on whether the victim gave clear consent. This difference can affect how cases are judged and how victims are supported.

**What happened** – On February 28, 2026, the European Parliament voted to ask the European Commission to make consent the main factor in all rape laws across the EU. This means that for something to be considered rape, there must be a clear, freely given “yes” from the person involved. Silence or not saying “no” is not enough to show consent. The vote was 447 in favor, 160 against, and 43 abstentions.

**Impact** – This decision is important because it changes the way rape is legally defined in many countries. It focuses on respecting a person’s choice and control over their own body. It also calls for better help for survivors, such as medical care, counseling, and safe abortion access. Right now, only 17 of the 27 EU countries have laws based on consent. This resolution encourages all countries to update their laws to protect victims better.

**What’s next step** – The European Commission will now consider creating a law that all EU countries would follow. Meanwhile, countries without consent-based laws can start changing their rules right away without waiting. This could lead to more consistent protection for people across Europe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament has taken a clear stand that only a freely given “yes” counts as consent, pushing all EU countries to update rape laws to better protect victims and respect personal choice.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen soutient des lois sur le viol basées sur le consentement dans toute l’UE
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans de nombreux pays de l’Union européenne (UE), les lois sur le viol varient. Certains définissent le viol par l’utilisation de la force ou des menaces, tandis que d’autres se concentrent sur le consentement clair de la personne. Cette différence influence la manière dont les cas sont jugés et la prise en charge des victimes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 28 février 2026, le Parlement européen a voté pour demander à la Commission européenne de faire du consentement le facteur principal dans toutes les lois sur le viol dans l’UE. Cela signifie que pour qu’un acte soit considéré comme un viol, il doit y avoir un « oui » clair et librement donné. Le silence ou le fait de ne pas dire « non » ne suffit pas à montrer un consentement. Le vote a été de 447 pour, 160 contre et 43 abstentions.

**Impact** – Cette décision est importante car elle change la définition légale du viol dans de nombreux pays. Elle met l’accent sur le respect du choix et du contrôle de la personne sur son propre corps. Elle appelle aussi à un meilleur soutien aux survivants, comme les soins médicaux, le soutien psychologique et l’accès à l’avortement sécurisé. Actuellement, seuls 17 des 27 pays de l’UE ont des lois basées sur le consentement. Cette résolution encourage tous les pays à mettre à jour leurs lois pour mieux protéger les victimes.

**Prochaine étape** – La Commission européenne va maintenant étudier la création d’une loi commune que tous les pays de l’UE devront suivre. En attendant, les pays sans lois basées sur le consentement peuvent commencer à modifier leurs règles sans attendre. Cela pourrait conduire à une meilleure protection uniforme des personnes en Europe.

**Phrase clé** – Le Parlement européen affirme clairement que seul un « oui » librement donné compte comme consentement, poussant tous les pays de l’UE à moderniser leurs lois sur le viol pour mieux protéger les victimes et respecter le choix personnel.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo impulsa leyes sobre violación basadas en el consentimiento en toda la UE
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En muchos países de la Unión Europea (UE), las leyes sobre violación son diferentes. Algunos definen la violación por el uso de la fuerza o amenazas, mientras que otros se basan en si la persona dio un consentimiento claro. Esta diferencia afecta cómo se juzgan los casos y cómo se apoya a las víctimas.

**Qué pasó** – El 28 de febrero de 2026, el Parlamento Europeo votó para pedir a la Comisión Europea que el consentimiento sea el factor principal en todas las leyes sobre violación en la UE. Esto significa que para que algo se considere violación, debe haber un “sí” claro y dado libremente por la persona. El silencio o no decir “no” no es suficiente para mostrar consentimiento. La votación fue 447 a favor, 160 en contra y 43 abstenciones.

**Impacto** – Esta decisión es importante porque cambia la forma en que se define legalmente la violación en muchos países. Se centra en respetar la elección y el control de la persona sobre su cuerpo. También pide mejor apoyo para las víctimas, como atención médica, ayuda psicológica y acceso a abortos seguros. Actualmente, solo 17 de los 27 países de la UE tienen leyes basadas en el consentimiento. Esta resolución anima a todos los países a actualizar sus leyes para proteger mejor a las víctimas.

**Próximo paso** – La Comisión Europea ahora estudiará crear una ley que todos los países de la UE deban seguir. Mientras tanto, los países sin leyes basadas en el consentimiento pueden empezar a cambiar sus normas sin esperar. Esto podría llevar a una protección más uniforme para las personas en Europa.

**Frase clave** – El Parlamento Europeo ha dejado claro que solo un “sí” dado libremente cuenta como consentimiento, impulsando a todos los países de la UE a actualizar las leyes sobre violación para proteger mejor a las víctimas y respetar la elección personal.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two hands gently clasping each other in a firm, supportive grip against a soft, neutral background, with a delicate, translucent ribbon flowing between them symbolizing clear, mutual consent and trust.

articles.title: US Court Upholds Right to Seek Asylum
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              “Mina” in Panama City, Panama, 2025.
                    © 2025 Human Rights Watch 
          
    


  
A panel of the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia rejected on April 24 the government’s denial of the right to seek asylum for asylum seekers who arrive at the southern border. The ruling stops summary deportations based on President Donald Trump’s Inauguration Day proclamation of an “invasion” at the US-Mexico border.The court held that US immigration law “makes plain that the right to apply for asylum is broadly available to all foreign individuals present or arriving in the United States unless expressly restricted from applying.”Last year, I traveled to Panama and Costa Rica to meet scores of asylum seekers from a Who’s Who of refugee-producing countries, including Russia, Iran, China, Afghanistan, and Cameroon. All arrived after Trump’s inauguration with the intent of seeking asylum; all were flatly denied that opportunity and were instead detained incommunicado, cuffed and chained, loaded onto planes, and dumped into countries with which they had no connections whatsoever.None of the people I interviewed, including those who gave harrowing accounts of persecution in their home countries, had the opportunity to apply for asylum. To implement Trump’s proclamation, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) issued guidance saying that individuals “who cross between the ports of entry on the southern land border” are “not permitted to apply for asylum.”And that’s exactly what happened. “I asked for asylum repeatedly… Nobody listened to me,” Mina, a 27-year-old Iranian woman who had converted to Christianity, told Human Rights Watch. “I didn’t understand why they didn’t listen to me. Then an immigration officer told me President Trump had ended asylum, so they were going to deport us.”The court has now ordered the DHS to stop deporting people like Mina, although the courts have so far not ruled that Mina and others like her who were denied access to the asylum process and wrongfully deported should be allowed to come back to apply for asylum.But going forward, the court’s message to DHS officials is clear: US immigration law “does not allow the President to remove Plaintiffs under summary removal procedures of his own making. Nor does it allow the Executive to suspend Plaintiffs’ right to apply for asylum.”
articles.content:
Click to expand Image “Mina” in Panama City, Panama, 2025. © 2025 Human Rights Watch

A panel of the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia rejected on April 24 the government’s denial of the right to seek asylum for asylum seekers who arrive at the southern border. The ruling stops summary deportations based on President Donald Trump’s Inauguration Day proclamation of an “invasion” at the US-Mexico border.

The court held that US immigration law “makes plain that the right to apply for asylum is broadly available to all foreign individuals present or arriving in the United States unless expressly restricted from applying.”

Last year, I traveled to Panama and Costa Rica to meet scores of asylum seekers from a Who’s Who of refugee-producing countries, including Russia, Iran, China, Afghanistan, and Cameroon. All arrived after Trump’s inauguration with the intent of seeking asylum; all were flatly denied that opportunity and were instead detained incommunicado, cuffed and chained, loaded onto planes, and dumped into countries with which they had no connections whatsoever.

None of the people I interviewed, including those who gave harrowing accounts of persecution in their home countries, had the opportunity to apply for asylum. To implement Trump’s proclamation, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) issued guidance saying that individuals “who cross between the ports of entry on the southern land border” are “not permitted to apply for asylum.”

And that’s exactly what happened.

“I asked for asylum repeatedly… Nobody listened to me,” Mina, a 27-year-old Iranian woman who had converted to Christianity, told Human Rights Watch. “I didn’t understand why they didn’t listen to me. Then an immigration officer told me President Trump had ended asylum, so they were going to deport us.”

The court has now ordered the DHS to stop deporting people like Mina, although the courts have so far not ruled that Mina and others like her who were denied access to the asylum process and wrongfully deported should be allowed to come back to apply for asylum.

But going forward, the court’s message to DHS officials is clear: US immigration law “does not allow the President to remove Plaintiffs under summary removal procedures of his own making. Nor does it allow the Executive to suspend Plaintiffs’ right to apply for asylum.”
articles.reasoning: The US Court of Appeals ruling upholds the legal right of asylum seekers to apply for asylum, stopping unlawful summary deportations. This decision protects vulnerable individuals fleeing persecution and reinforces the rule of law in immigration policy, representing a significant positive impact on human rights with broad societal implications.
articles.personality_title: US court protects right to seek asylum at the southern border
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In recent years, many people arriving at the US southern border seeking safety were quickly sent back without a chance to ask for asylum. This happened after a government rule said those crossing between official points could not apply for asylum.

**What happened** – On April 24, a US Court of Appeals panel ruled against this government policy. It said that under US law, anyone arriving in the country can apply for asylum unless clearly stated otherwise. The court stopped the government from sending people back without letting them try to stay.

**Impact** – This ruling protects people fleeing danger in their home countries by giving them a fair chance to ask for safety in the US. It also makes clear that the President cannot ignore immigration laws by creating quick deportation rules. For example, Mina, a 27-year-old woman from Iran, was previously denied asylum and told she would be deported, but now such actions must stop.

**What's next step** – The Department of Homeland Security must follow this ruling and allow asylum seekers to apply. Although the court has not decided if people already deported can return to apply, this decision will change how asylum requests are handled in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The US court has confirmed that everyone arriving at the southern border has the legal right to seek asylum and cannot be sent away without a proper chance to apply.
articles.personality_title_fr: La cour américaine protège le droit de demander l'asile à la frontière sud
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Ces dernières années, de nombreuses personnes arrivant à la frontière sud des États-Unis pour chercher refuge étaient rapidement renvoyées sans pouvoir demander l'asile. Cela résultait d'une règle gouvernementale interdisant la demande d'asile aux personnes traversant entre les points d'entrée officiels.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 24 avril, un panel de la Cour d'appel américaine a annulé cette politique. Il a déclaré que selon la loi américaine, toute personne arrivant dans le pays peut demander l'asile, sauf indication contraire claire. La cour a empêché le gouvernement de renvoyer des gens sans leur permettre d'essayer de rester.

**Impact** – Cette décision protège les personnes fuyant des dangers dans leur pays en leur donnant une vraie chance de demander la sécurité aux États-Unis. Elle rappelle aussi que le président ne peut pas ignorer la loi sur l'immigration en créant des règles rapides d'expulsion. Par exemple, Mina, une femme iranienne de 27 ans, s'était vu refuser l'asile et menacée d'expulsion, mais désormais ces pratiques doivent cesser.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Département de la Sécurité intérieure doit respecter cette décision et permettre aux demandeurs d'asile de faire leur demande. Même si la cour n'a pas encore décidé si les personnes déjà expulsées peuvent revenir, cette décision va changer la manière dont les demandes d'asile sont traitées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La cour américaine a confirmé que toute personne arrivant à la frontière sud a le droit légal de demander l'asile et ne peut pas être renvoyée sans une vraie chance de déposer sa demande.
articles.personality_title_es: Tribunal de EE.UU. protege el derecho a solicitar asilo en la frontera sur
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En los últimos años, muchas personas que llegaban a la frontera sur de Estados Unidos buscando protección eran devueltas rápidamente sin poder solicitar asilo. Esto ocurrió debido a una regla que prohibía pedir asilo a quienes cruzaban entre puntos oficiales.

**Qué pasó** – El 24 de abril, un panel del Tribunal de Apelaciones de EE.UU. anuló esta política. Dijo que según la ley estadounidense, cualquier persona que llegue al país puede solicitar asilo, a menos que se diga claramente lo contrario. El tribunal detuvo que el gobierno expulsara a personas sin darles la oportunidad de quedarse.

**Impacto** – Esta decisión protege a quienes huyen de peligros en sus países al darles una oportunidad justa para pedir seguridad en EE.UU. También deja claro que el presidente no puede ignorar las leyes de inmigración creando reglas rápidas de deportación. Por ejemplo, Mina, una mujer iraní de 27 años, antes fue negada y amenazada con deportación, pero ahora estas acciones deben parar.

**Próximo paso** – El Departamento de Seguridad Nacional debe cumplir esta decisión y permitir que los solicitantes de asilo hagan sus pedidos. Aunque el tribunal aún no ha decidido si los deportados pueden regresar, esta medida cambiará cómo se manejan las solicitudes de asilo.

**Una frase clave** – El tribunal de EE.UU. confirmó que todas las personas que llegan a la frontera sur tienen derecho legal a pedir asilo y no pueden ser expulsadas sin una oportunidad real para solicitarlo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an open border gate between two softly colored landscapes, with a glowing, gently flowing river symbolizing the path to asylum, and a group of diverse, faceless silhouettes carrying simple belongings walking peacefully toward the gate under a calm sky.

articles.title: US: Allow Those With Felony Convictions to Vote
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              Spellman Bernard Smith, Jr., 78, center, stands in line with other voters at Rosemont Middle School in Norfolk, Virginia, on Election Day, November 8, 2016, to cast his vote. Smith, who has a felony conviction, recently had his voting rights restored and was able to cast a vote for president for the first time. 
                    © 2016 Bill Tiernan/The Virginian-Pilot via AP
          
    


  
(New York) – Legislation advancing in Maryland and Alabama would expand voting rights for people with felony convictions, marking significant progress in the movement to expand voting rights for all, Human Rights Watch said today. Alabama recently enacted a law to make it easier for people to vote after they complete prison sentences. Maryland Governor Wes Moore should sign HB115 into law, and other states should follow their lead. In Maryland, HB115 would automatically register people to vote as they are leaving prison, removing a significant barrier that has kept eligible voters off the rolls for years. Alabama’s Senate Bill 24, recently signed by the governor, requires the Board of Pardons and Paroles to provide “easily accessible” information regarding the restoration process to people who have completed their sentences. It also requires the board to inform the secretary of state when individual rights are restored to add them to the voter rolls. The bill’s passage marks significant progress for the state, where 1 in 10 Black adults are disenfranchised because of criminal convictions. “These bills are hard-won victories by advocates and directly affected people who have worked for years to expand the right to vote in these states,” said Trey Walk, democracy researcher and advocate at Human Rights Watch. “The United States should live up to its democratic ideals by offering the right to vote without regard to a person’s experience with the criminal legal system.”These reforms come at the same time as the Trump administration has taken steps that could make it more difficult to vote. The administration has sought to impose new restrictions on vote-by-mail, which millions of voters, including people with disabilities, rely on. In October 2025, the US Supreme Court heard arguments in Louisiana v. Callais, which asks whether creating majority-Black congressional districts, to comply with Section 2 of the 1965 Voting Rights Act, violates the US Constitution. That case, which will be decided in the coming months, has drawn serious alarm from racial justice and voting rights advocates. “With sustained attacks on federal voting rights protections, it is critical for state legislatures to take up the mantle,” Walk said. “They should not only defend but expand access to the ballot.” The United States remains out of step with prevailing global practices in disenfranchising large numbers of people based on criminal convictions, with felony disenfranchisement affecting over four million people.This impact is highly racialized. The Sentencing Project estimated in 2024 that 1 in 22 Black Americans of voting age is disenfranchised, more than 3 times the rate of people who are not Black. According to the same study, at least 495,000 US Latines, or 1.5 percent of the voting-eligible population, are disenfranchised.More work will remain even if both bills become law, Human Rights Watch said. Alabama’s bill in particular would retain the state’s practice of requiring people on felony parole or probation to pay court fees, fines, and other costs before their voting rights can be restored. This kind of “pay-to-vote” requirement disproportionately keeps low-income people off the voting rolls.Globally, most countries with populations of 1.5 million or more never, or rarely, deny the right to vote because of a criminal conviction. The reforms in states like Maryland and Alabama move the United States closer to this international norm.The right to vote is protected under the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights, which the United States ratified in 1992. The United Nations Human Rights Committee holds that any restrictions on voting rights must be “objective and reasonable” and that the length of any suspension of voting rights based on a conviction should be proportionate to the offense and the sentence. In 2024, Human Rights Watch, along with the Sentencing Project and the American Civil Liberties Union, released “Out of Step: U.S. Policy on Voting Rights in Global Perspective,” a report examining state and federal policies regarding felony disenfranchisement. The groups recommended that the United States should end felony disenfranchisement, eliminate “pay-to-vote” practices, establish polling centers in correctional facilities, and require officials to notify all eligible voters of their rights.“Every state should be working to tear down unnecessary barriers to voting,” Walk said. “Voting is a human right that protects everyone.”
articles.content:
Click to expand Image Spellman Bernard Smith, Jr., 78, center, stands in line with other voters at Rosemont Middle School in Norfolk, Virginia, on Election Day, November 8, 2016, to cast his vote. Smith, who has a felony conviction, recently had his voting rights restored and was able to cast a vote for president for the first time. © 2016 Bill Tiernan/The Virginian-Pilot via AP

(New York) – Legislation advancing in Maryland and Alabama would expand voting rights for people with felony convictions, marking significant progress in the movement to expand voting rights for all, Human Rights Watch said today. Alabama recently enacted a law to make it easier for people to vote after they complete prison sentences. Maryland Governor Wes Moore should sign HB115 into law, and other states should follow their lead.

In Maryland, HB115 would automatically register people to vote as they are leaving prison, removing a significant barrier that has kept eligible voters off the rolls for years. Alabama’s Senate Bill 24, recently signed by the governor, requires the Board of Pardons and Paroles to provide “easily accessible” information regarding the restoration process to people who have completed their sentences. It also requires the board to inform the secretary of state when individual rights are restored to add them to the voter rolls. The bill’s passage marks significant progress for the state, where 1 in 10 Black adults are disenfranchised because of criminal convictions.

“These bills are hard-won victories by advocates and directly affected people who have worked for years to expand the right to vote in these states,” said Trey Walk, democracy researcher and advocate at Human Rights Watch. “The United States should live up to its democratic ideals by offering the right to vote without regard to a person’s experience with the criminal legal system.”

These reforms come at the same time as the Trump administration has taken steps that could make it more difficult to vote. The administration has sought to impose new restrictions on vote-by-mail, which millions of voters, including people with disabilities, rely on.

In October 2025, the US Supreme Court heard arguments in Louisiana v. Callais, which asks whether creating majority-Black congressional districts, to comply with Section 2 of the 1965 Voting Rights Act, violates the US Constitution. That case, which will be decided in the coming months, has drawn serious alarm from racial justice and voting rights advocates.

“With sustained attacks on federal voting rights protections, it is critical for state legislatures to take up the mantle,” Walk said. “They should not only defend but expand access to the ballot.”

The United States remains out of step with prevailing global practices in disenfranchising large numbers of people based on criminal convictions, with felony disenfranchisement affecting over four million people.

This impact is highly racialized. The Sentencing Project estimated in 2024 that 1 in 22 Black Americans of voting age is disenfranchised, more than 3 times the rate of people who are not Black. According to the same study, at least 495,000 US Latines, or 1.5 percent of the voting-eligible population, are disenfranchised.

More work will remain even if both bills become law, Human Rights Watch said. Alabama’s bill in particular would retain the state’s practice of requiring people on felony parole or probation to pay court fees, fines, and other costs before their voting rights can be restored. This kind of “pay-to-vote” requirement disproportionately keeps low-income people off the voting rolls.

Globally, most countries with populations of 1.5 million or more never, or rarely, deny the right to vote because of a criminal conviction. The reforms in states like Maryland and Alabama move the United States closer to this international norm.

The right to vote is protected under the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights, which the United States ratified in 1992. The United Nations Human Rights Committee holds that any restrictions on voting rights must be “objective and reasonable” and that the length of any suspension of voting rights based on a conviction should be proportionate to the offense and the sentence.

In 2024, Human Rights Watch, along with the Sentencing Project and the American Civil Liberties Union, released “Out of Step: U.S. Policy on Voting Rights in Global Perspective,” a report examining state and federal policies regarding felony disenfranchisement. The groups recommended that the United States should end felony disenfranchisement, eliminate “pay-to-vote” practices, establish polling centers in correctional facilities, and require officials to notify all eligible voters of their rights.

“Every state should be working to tear down unnecessary barriers to voting,” Walk said. “Voting is a human right that protects everyone.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports significant legislative progress in Maryland and Alabama expanding voting rights for people with felony convictions, addressing a major civil rights issue affecting millions, particularly racial minorities. The reforms remove barriers to voting, promote democratic inclusion, and align U.S. policy closer to international human rights standards. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights broad societal impact.
articles.personality_title: Maryland and Alabama expand voting rights for people with felony convictions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the United States, many people with felony convictions lose their right to vote, sometimes even after finishing their prison sentences. This affects millions of people, especially Black and Latino communities.

**What happened** – Maryland and Alabama have passed new laws to help people with felony convictions regain their voting rights. Maryland’s law, HB115, would automatically register people to vote when they leave prison. Alabama’s Senate Bill 24 requires officials to give clear information about how to restore voting rights and to add those people back to the voter rolls once their rights are restored.

**Impact** – These laws make it easier for thousands of people to participate in elections, especially in Alabama where 1 in 10 Black adults cannot vote because of past convictions. The changes reduce unfair barriers and bring the U.S. closer to other countries that rarely stop people from voting because of criminal records.

**What’s next step** – Maryland’s governor still needs to sign HB115 into law. Other states may follow by creating similar rules. Advocacy groups want to end requirements that force people to pay fines before they can vote again, as this keeps many low-income people from voting.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New laws in Maryland and Alabama are helping people with felony convictions regain their voting rights, making elections fairer and more inclusive.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Maryland et l'Alabama élargissent le droit de vote aux personnes condamnées pour un crime
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Aux États-Unis, de nombreuses personnes condamnées pour un crime perdent leur droit de vote, parfois même après avoir purgé leur peine. Cela touche des millions de personnes, surtout dans les communautés noires et latino-américaines.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le Maryland et l'Alabama ont adopté de nouvelles lois pour aider ces personnes à retrouver leur droit de vote. La loi du Maryland, HB115, inscrit automatiquement les personnes sur les listes électorales à leur sortie de prison. En Alabama, la loi Senate Bill 24 oblige les autorités à fournir des informations claires sur la procédure de restauration des droits et à inscrire ces personnes sur les listes électorales.

**Impact** – Ces lois facilitent la participation électorale de milliers de personnes, notamment en Alabama où 1 adulte noir sur 10 ne peut pas voter à cause de condamnations passées. Ces changements réduisent les obstacles injustes et rapprochent les États-Unis d’autres pays qui refusent rarement le droit de vote pour des condamnations pénales.

**Prochaine étape** – Le gouverneur du Maryland doit encore signer la loi HB115. D’autres États pourraient suivre cet exemple. Les groupes de défense souhaitent supprimer les exigences de paiement des amendes avant la restauration du droit de vote, car cela empêche beaucoup de personnes à faibles revenus de voter.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouvelles lois au Maryland et en Alabama aident les personnes condamnées à retrouver leur droit de vote, rendant les élections plus justes et inclusives.
articles.personality_title_es: Maryland y Alabama amplían el derecho al voto para personas con condenas penales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Estados Unidos, muchas personas con condenas penales pierden su derecho a votar, incluso después de cumplir sus sentencias. Esto afecta a millones, especialmente a las comunidades negras y latinas.

**Qué pasó** – Maryland y Alabama aprobaron nuevas leyes para ayudar a estas personas a recuperar su derecho al voto. La ley HB115 de Maryland registra automáticamente a las personas para votar al salir de prisión. La Ley del Senado 24 de Alabama exige que las autoridades informen claramente sobre el proceso de restauración y agreguen a estas personas a las listas electorales.

**Impacto** – Estas leyes facilitan que miles de personas participen en las elecciones, especialmente en Alabama, donde 1 de cada 10 adultos negros está privado del voto por condenas pasadas. Los cambios reducen barreras injustas y acercan a EE.UU. a otros países que rara vez niegan el voto por antecedentes penales.

**Próximo paso** – El gobernador de Maryland aún debe firmar la HB115. Otros estados podrían seguir su ejemplo. Los grupos de defensa buscan eliminar los requisitos de pago de multas antes de restaurar el derecho al voto, ya que esto impide que muchas personas con bajos ingresos voten.

**Frase clave** – Nuevas leyes en Maryland y Alabama ayudan a personas con condenas penales a recuperar su derecho al voto, haciendo las elecciones más justas e inclusivas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a diverse group of silhouetted hands reaching together to place ballots into a large, open ballot box shaped like a restored chain link, symbolizing the unlocking of voting rights for people with felony convictions, set against a softly glowing background of interconnected voting districts outlined in natural, muted earth tones.
